title: Linked List Data Structures
description: A linked list is a linear data structure where elements are not stored at contiguous memory locations. We will explore Singly, Doubly, and Circular Linked Lists, where elements are linked using forward and sometimes backward pointers.

sections.title: 1. Singly Linked List
sections.content: In a Singly Linked List, each 'Node' contains the data and a single pointer to the 'Next' node in the sequence. Traversal is strictly one-way (from Head to Tail). The final node points to null.

sections.title: 2. Doubly Linked List
sections.content: A Doubly Linked List adds a 'Prev' pointer to each node, linking it to the previous node. This allows for two-way bidirectional traversal, making operations like reversing the list or deleting a specific node much more efficient, at the cost of extra memory for the second pointer.

sections.title: 3. Circular Linked List
sections.content: In a Circular Linked List, the Tail node does not point to null. Instead, its 'Next' pointer loops back and points directly to the Head node, forming a closed circle. This can be implemented as a singly or doubly circular list and is excellent for continuous looping.

sections.title: Real-World Applications
sections.content: Singly lists handle hash map collisions. Doubly lists are perfect for Browser History (Forward/Back) and Undo/Redo operations. Circular lists are crucial for Operating System CPU scheduling (Round Robin algorithms) and multiplayer board game turn management.
